brief_document.title: Command, Resolution, and the Resolver
brief_document.purpose: Define the seam through which every world-touching workflow call is decided. The Command and Resolution types are the vocabulary the engine and the durability layer share; the Resolver is the single chokepoint that, against the HistoryCursor, returns a recorded Resolution, reports resume-live, or (in AD-005) raises a violation. Funnelling all calls through one place means recorded-vs-live is decided in exactly one location.
brief_document.task: Define the Command and Resolution types (command.rs) and the Resolver (resolver.rs). A Command models each world-touching call (run activity, await signal, start timer, spawn child, complete workflow), each carrying its CorrelationKey and any input Payload. A Resolution models each recorded outcome. The Resolver holds a HistoryCursor and, given a Command, maps the cursor's Matched into a Resolution, the cursor's Exhausted into ResumeLive, and (wiring left to AD-005) the cursor's Mismatch into a violation. This brief delivers the happy-path resolution (Matched -> Resolution, Exhausted -> ResumeLive) and the single entry point; AD-005 fills in the Mismatch -> NonDeterminismError path and the dedicated tests.

requirements.title: Command type
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a Command enum modelling the world-touching calls a workflow can issue: RunActivity, AwaitSignal, StartTimer, SpawnChild, and CompleteWorkflow, each carrying its CorrelationKey and (where applicable) its input Payload and activity/workflow type.
acceptance: command.rs defines Command with variants RunActivity { key, activity_type, input }, AwaitSignal { key }, StartTimer { key, fire_at }, SpawnChild { key, workflow_type, input }, CompleteWorkflow { result }.
Each variant's key is a CorrelationKey (from AD-003) consistent with the recorded event it will be matched against.
A unit test constructs one Command of each variant and asserts its CorrelationKey round-trips.

requirements.title: Resolution type
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a Resolution enum modelling the recorded outcomes a Command can resolve to: ActivityCompleted(result Payload), ActivityFailedTerminal(ActivityError), TimerFired, SignalDelivered(payload Payload), ChildCompleted(result Payload), and ChildFailed(WorkflowError).
acceptance: command.rs (or a sibling) defines Resolution with the six variants above plus a ResumeLive sentinel (or ResumeLive is a distinct return value alongside Resolution — pick one and document it).
Each recorded-outcome Resolution carries the aion-core domain value drawn from the recorded event (Payload / ActivityError / WorkflowError).
A unit test constructs each Resolution variant and asserts equality round-trips.

requirements.title: Resolver: Matched -> Resolution, Exhausted -> ResumeLive
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a Resolver holding a HistoryCursor. WHEN given a Command, THE Resolver SHALL consult the cursor for the matching family and key: on Matched it SHALL return the corresponding recorded Resolution; on Exhausted it SHALL return ResumeLive.
acceptance: resolver.rs defines Resolver owning a HistoryCursor (and a reference to the DeterminismContext is wired in AD-006; this brief may take it as a parameter or leave a documented hook).
resolve(command) maps a cursor Matched(recorded events) to the Resolution carrying the recorded domain value (e.g. ActivityCompleted's result Payload).
resolve(command) maps a cursor Exhausted to ResumeLive.

A unit test resolves a RunActivity against a history containing its ActivityCompleted and asserts Resolution::ActivityCompleted with the recorded Payload; a second RunActivity past the recorded end asserts ResumeLive.

The Mismatch arm is present and, in this brief, returns a typed Err (DurabilityError::NonDeterminism with a NonDeterminismError built from the cursor's expected/found descriptors) — it MUST NOT be todo!()/unimplemented!()/unreachable!()/panic! (all clippy-denied per CO2) and MUST NOT silently fall through to a Resolution. AD-005 enriches the violation path and adds the dedicated tests; the interim behaviour is already a real, returned error.

requirements.title: Single chokepoint for all world-touching calls
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL route every world-touching call through the single Resolver::resolve entry point so that recorded-vs-live is decided in exactly one location. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT provide an alternative path that resolves a Command outside the Resolver.
acceptance: There is exactly one public method (resolve) that maps a Command to a resolution outcome; no per-family resolve_activity / resolve_timer public methods exist that bypass it.
The activity, timer, signal, and child families are all handled inside resolve via the CorrelationKey, not via separate public entry points.
A unit test exercises resolve for one Command of each family against a matching history and asserts the correct Resolution variant for each.

boundaries: SHALL NOT complete the Mismatch -> NonDeterminismError logic or its dedicated tests — that is AD-005 (this brief leaves a single, compiling, non-silent delegation point).
SHALL NOT invoke any LiveExecutor — ResumeLive is a returned signal, not a live call; the handoff is AD-007.
SHALL NOT read the wall clock or generate randomness — determinism inputs are AD-006.
verification: cargo test -p aion passes (command + resolver happy-path tests).
Exactly one public resolve entry point exists (CO19/C19) — grep confirms no bypassing per-family public resolvers.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: A Command enum models the world-touching calls the workflow can issue: RunActivity, AwaitSignal, StartTimer, SpawnChild, plus workflow-completion intent, each carrying its correlation key and any input Payload.
A Resolution enum models recorded outcomes: ActivityCompleted(result), ActivityFailedTerminal(error), TimerFired, SignalDelivered(payload), ChildCompleted(result), ChildFailed(error).
The Resolver, given a Command, returns exactly one of: a recorded Resolution from the cursor, ResumeLive when the cursor is exhausted, or a NonDeterminismError on family/key mismatch.
All world-touching calls funnel through the single Resolver entry point; recorded-vs-live and violation are decided in exactly one place.

stories.text: As the engine, I want to replay a workflow from its recorded history and have every activity, timer, signal, and child call return its recorded outcome so that I reconstruct the exact prior state without repeating side effects.
As a workflow author, I want replay to be invisible so that I write a normal deterministic function and never code differently for the replay case.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny across the whole module set.
No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] bypasses; tests use InMemoryStore in-process, never env-gated skips.
mod.rs holds only pub mod declarations and re-exports; logic lives in named files.
500-line file limit (excluding tests/comments/whitespace); split the Resolver and replay driver before they exceed it.
aion::durability depends only on aion-core and aion-store (plus an RNG crate and chrono); no AT, AF, networking, or direct beamr — runtime concerns are reached only through the LiveExecutor trait.

Single-writer discipline is structural: only a Recorder appends to a workflow's history, exactly one Recorder per active workflow; no other path calls EventStore::append for a workflow with a live Recorder.

Every append states an expected_seq from the Recorder's tracked head; the head is never guessed or read from outside the Recorder, and a SequenceConflict is surfaced as a hard error (double-writer bug), not retried by re-reading.

workflow.now derives only from the recorded event timestamp in the DeterminismContext; no code path here calls the wall clock to produce a workflow-visible value. The recovery wall time for expired_timers is explicitly not workflow-visible.
workflow.random derives only from the seed (WorkflowId + RunId); no entropy source is consulted; same run -> identical random sequence.
A non-determinism violation is always raised as a typed NonDeterminismError at the point of mismatch; never swallowed, never guessed past, never allowed to produce a Resolution.
Replay never invokes a live side effect: while the cursor can satisfy a command the LiveExecutor is not called; it is called only after ResumeLive.
Determinism-violation detection has dedicated test coverage: a recorded history that deliberately diverges from the replayed command stream asserts the typed error.

resolved_context.intention: Make Aion durable. Everything else in the engine is mechanics a non-durable orchestrator could also have; this layer is what lets a workflow crash on step nine of ten and resume exactly where it was, sleep for three months and resume in the same logical state, and run as if it ran once even after five replays. It does three things: it records (every externally-observable engine action becomes an Event appended to the EventStore under a single-writer-per-workflow discipline with an expected-sequence guard); it replays (re-runs the workflow function from the start, and for every world-touching call consults recorded history and returns the recorded outcome instead of acting again); and it enforces determinism (supplies the recorded timestamp for workflow.now, a workflow-seeded value for workflow.random, and detects when a replay diverges from recorded history — failing loudly rather than corrupting state silently). When done, the engine (AE) spawns a workflow process and hands it a resolver that transparently answers every world-touching call from history during replay and falls through to live execution at the resume point; recovery becomes list_active, replay each, resume. The workflow author writes a plain deterministic function and never knows whether a call executed for real or returned a cached result.

provenance.quote: Make Aion durable. Everything else in the engine is mechanics a non-durable orchestrator could also have; this layer is what lets a workflow crash on step nine of ten and resume exactly where it was,
